agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA display from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). In logistics and courier operations, ETA precision is a core customer-facing promise that directly drives recipient planning behavior, sender confidence, and SLA accountability. Reducing granularity from hour-level to day-level is not a cosmetic UI change — it fundamentally alters the service promise.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking & ETA Presentation","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the predicted moment a package will be delivered to the recipient. In courier/logistics, this is the primary customer-facing commitment."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"A bounded time range within which delivery is promised. Can be exact (timestamp), hourly (2-hour window), or date-level (by end of day)."},{"term":"SLA (Service Level Agreement)","definition":"Contractual or published commitment defining delivery speed and precision. Often tied to service tier (standard, express, same-day)."},{"term":"Service Tier","definition":"A classification of delivery speed/priority (e.g., standard, express, same-day, next-day) that determines pricing and ETA precision expectations."},{"term":"Last Mile","definition":"The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to recipient address. The most variable and hardest-to-predict segment."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"The person or entity receiving the delivery, whose planning depends on ETA accuracy."},{"term":"Sender","definition":"The customer or business that originated the shipment and pays for the service tier."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"ETA precision must match the service tier purchased. Express and same-day tiers inherently promise time-level precision; showing only a date violates the service commitment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If a customer pays for same-day or time-definite delivery, the ETA must reflect that precision. A date-only ETA for a same-day service is logically meaningless ('Delivery by today' with no time) and breaks the fundamental value proposition.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment with an express or time-definite service tier, when the ETA is displayed, then it MUST include a time component (timestamp or time window), not just a date."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Any change to ETA display format must preserve the ability to measure and enforce SLA compliance.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"SLA compliance is contractual. If ETAs lose time precision, the system can no longer determine whether a 'by 14:00' SLA was met or missed, corrupting operational accountability.","testable_assertion":"Given an SLA defined with time-level precision, when delivery occurs, then the system MUST still be able to evaluate on-time vs late against the original SLA terms."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"ETA granularity must not be less precise than the granularity of the customer's delivery commitment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Displaying a less-precise ETA than what was promised creates a mismatch between service expectation and information provided, eroding trust and creating disputes.","testable_assertion":"Given a delivery commitment at hour-level precision, when ETA is rendered, then the displayed ETA must be at least hour-level precise."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"For standard/economy service tiers where no time-level commitment exists, date-only ETAs may be acceptable.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy decision. A VP could decide standard tiers show date-only or date+time based on competitive positioning.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment with a standard service tier and no time-level SLA, when ETA is displayed, then a date-only format is permissible."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"ETA format changes must not break downstream integrations that consume timestamp-precision ETAs (e.g., webhook payloads, partner APIs, reporting).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"External systems and partners may depend on timestamp-precision ETA data. Removing the time component from API responses would be a breaking change.","testable_assertion":"Given an external system consuming ETA data via API, when ETA format changes in the UI, then API payloads MUST continue to include full timestamp precision."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A shipment with a time-definite service tier (express, same-day, scheduled)","when":"The recipient or sender views the delivery ETA","then":"The ETA MUST display with time-level precision (timestamp or time window), not date-only"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A shipment with a standard/economy service tier and no time-level SLA","when":"The recipient or sender views the delivery ETA","then":"The ETA MAY display as date-only (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday')"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Any service tier","when":"The ETA is served via API to external consumers or partners","then":"The response payload MUST retain full timestamp precision regardless of UI display changes"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An SLA-tracked shipment where the original SLA includes a time component","when":"The shipment is delivered and SLA compliance is evaluated","then":"The system evaluates compliance against the original time-precise SLA, not the date-only display"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A sender who purchased express delivery","when":"They check the tracking page and see a date-only ETA","then":"This is a DEFECT — the display contradicts the service they purchased"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Same-day delivery showing 'Delivery by Today' with no time","expected_behavior":"Must show a time window (e.g., 'by 6:00 PM today'). Date-only is meaningless for same-day — the recipient cannot plan around 'today' without a time."},{"scenario":"Cross-timezone deliveries where 'Friday' is ambiguous","expected_behavior":"Date-only display must clarify the timezone or use the recipient's local date. 'Delivery by Friday' must mean Friday in the recipient's timezone."},{"scenario":"Shipment crosses midnight during last-mile transit","expected_behavior":"If ETA was 'by Friday' and the package arrives at 00:15 Saturday, is that late? Date-only creates ambiguity about the SLA boundary that timestamps avoid."},{"scenario":"B2B customers with receiving dock hours","expected_behavior":"Business recipients with limited receiving windows need time-level ETAs to staff their docks. Date-only would force them to staff all day."},{"scenario":"Multiple deliveries to same address on same date","expected_behavior":"Date-only ETAs make it impossible for recipients to distinguish between two packages arriving the same day at different times."},{"scenario":"Perishable or temperature-sensitive goods","expected_behavior":"Recipient must know approximate arrival time to ensure someone is available. Date-only ETA for perishables risks spoilage if recipient is away for hours."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Significant risk. Express and premium tiers command higher prices BECAUSE of time precision. If ETAs become date-only across the board, customers lose a key reason to pay premium rates, potentially compressing margins. SLA dispute resolution also becomes harder — ambiguous delivery windows increase refund/credit claims because 'by Friday' is harder to prove met than 'by 14:30 Friday'. Estimated impact: increased SLA dispute costs and potential premium-tier revenue erosion.","operational":"Mixed. Date-only ETAs reduce the pressure on operations to hit exact time targets, which could lower last-mile routing complexity and driver stress. However, it also removes a key operational feedback signal — if the system no longer commits to times, there is less incentive to optimize route efficiency. Internal SLA dashboards and performance metrics that rely on timestamp precision would need rework or would lose fidelity.","user":"Negative for most segments. Recipients plan their day around delivery times — taking time off work, waiting at home, scheduling someone to receive packages. Removing time precision forces recipients into all-day availability windows, which is a material downgrade in experience. Sender confidence also drops: businesses shipping to their customers lose the ability to promise precise delivery, weakening their own customer experience. For standard-tier low-urgency shipments, the impact is minimal.","risk":"High. (1) Contractual risk: existing B2B contracts may specify time-level SLAs — changing display could trigger breach claims. (2) Competitive risk: major carriers (UPS, FedEx, Amazon) are moving TOWARD more precise ETAs, not less. Going date-only moves against industry direction. (3) Compliance risk: some regulated shipments (pharma, legal documents) may require time-precise delivery records. (4) Data integrity risk: if the backend also loses time precision (not just UI), historical analytics and ML models for ETA prediction lose training signal quality.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Recipients (end consumers)","impact":"Loss of delivery time visibility. Must plan for all-day availability instead of a specific window. Material experience downgrade for express/same-day."},{"group":"Senders (businesses/shippers)","impact":"Cannot promise time-precise delivery to their own customers. Weakens their value proposition, especially for e-commerce where delivery speed is a competitive differentiator."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Reduced precision pressure could ease routing, but also removes optimization incentive. SLA measurement becomes ambiguous."},{"group":"B2B / Enterprise customers","impact":"Dock scheduling, staffing, and receiving workflows depend on time-level ETAs. Date-only would increase their operational costs."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Likely increase in 'where is my package' contacts. Vague ETAs generate more inbound inquiries because recipients lack information to self-serve."},{"group":"Partner / API consumers","impact":"Any downstream system consuming ETAs must not receive degraded data. Breaking change risk if API responses also lose precision."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue","impact":"Risk of premium-tier devaluation and increased SLA dispute credits."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The proposal lacks critical context: we do not know which service tiers exist in this system, whether this change applies to UI-only or also backend/API, what motivated the change (cost reduction? accuracy issues?), whether existing SLA contracts specify time precision, or what customer segments are served. Without this context, the analysis is based on general logistics domain knowledge and could miss system-specific rules. The confidence is low because the business justification and scope of the change were never stated.","high_confidence_areas":["Time-definite service tiers must retain time-precision ETAs — this is an industry invariant","API/integration payloads must not lose precision regardless of UI changes","Recipient experience is degraded when time precision is removed for premium tiers"],"low_confidence_areas":["What service tiers exist in this specific system","Whether any B2B contracts mandate time-level ETAs","What motivated this proposal — accuracy problems? cost reduction? simplification?","Whether this is UI-only or also affects API responses and backend storage","What percentage of shipments are standard vs express tier"],"assumptions":["The system supports multiple service tiers with varying delivery speed commitments","Express/same-day tiers exist and carry time-level SLA expectations","External API consumers exist who receive ETA data","The proposal intends to change the customer-facing display, not necessarily the backend ETA calculation"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"The proposal does not state WHICH service tiers are affected by this change. If the system only handles standard ground shipping with no time-level SLAs, date-only ETAs may be acceptable. If express/same-day tiers exist, this change could violate service commitments. We need the full list of service tiers and their SLA definitions.","affected_stakeholders":["Senders","Recipients","Operations","Finance"],"options":["Apply date-only to ALL tiers (high risk if premium tiers exist)","Apply date-only only to standard/economy tiers (safe if time-definite tiers retain timestamps)","Keep timestamps everywhere but add date-only as a fallback when prediction confidence is low"],"recommendation":"Clarify which service tiers exist and which are in scope for this change before proceeding."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The motivation for this change was never stated. Common reasons include: (a) ETA predictions are frequently inaccurate at timestamp level, causing customer dissatisfaction; (b) engineering simplification; (c) reducing SLA penalty exposure. The correct solution differs dramatically based on the root cause. If predictions are inaccurate, the fix might be better ML models, not less precision.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Operations","Recipients"],"options":["If accuracy-driven: improve prediction models rather than hiding imprecision","If cost-driven: selectively reduce precision for low-value tiers only","If simplification-driven: evaluate whether the complexity reduction justifies the experience downgrade"],"recommendation":"Product and engineering must state the business rationale before domain approval can be given."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Scope of change is undefined: is this UI-only (display layer) or does it also affect API responses, webhook payloads, backend storage, and SLA evaluation logic? The business impact differs by orders of magnitude between a display-only change and a system-wide precision reduction.","affected_stakeholders":["Partner / API consumers","Engineering","Operations"],"options":["UI-only change with backend retaining full precision (lower risk)","Full-stack change removing time precision everywhere (very high risk)"],"recommendation":"Confirm this is UI-only. If backend or API precision is also changing, this requires a much deeper impact analysis and likely a versioned API migration."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information provided about existing contractual SLA obligations with B2B or enterprise customers. If contracts specify time-definite delivery commitments, changing ETA display could trigger breach of contract claims or require contract renegotiation.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B / Enterprise customers","Legal","Finance"],"options":["Review all active B2B contracts for time-precision requirements before proceeding","Exempt B2B customers from the change entirely"],"recommendation":"Legal and account management must review B2B contracts for time-precision obligations before this change ships."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Blanket application of date-only ETAs across all service tiers would misrepresent the service commitment for time-definite tiers","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: ETA precision must match the service tier purchased"},{"violation":"Without confirming scope, this change risks degrading API payloads consumed by partners and downstream systems","business_rule_broken":"BR-5: ETA format changes must not break downstream integrations"}]}

In logistics and courier operations, ETA precision is a core customer-facing promise that directly drives recipient planning behavior, sender confidence, and SLA accountability. Reducing granularity from hour-level to day-level is not a cosmetic UI change — it fundamentally alters the ser

content.thinking:
Analyzing the business impact of changing delivery ETAs from exact timestamps to date-only windows.

This is a domain analysis task about ETA presentation in a logistics/courier context. Let me think through this carefully.

Key considerations:
- Moving from precise timestamps to date-only windows is a significant reduction in information granularity
- This affects customer expectations, SLA measurement, operational planning, and potentially contractual obligations

Let me classify: The scenario describes a proposed change but doesn't explain WHY the team wants this change, what problems the current exact timestamps cause, what service types exist, whether there are contractual SLA obligations tied to timestamps, or what customer segments are affected. 

I'm noticing that several critical details are missing from the original request—the motivation behind this change, the specific pain points with current timestamps, service tier definitions, any contractual SLA constraints, and which customer segments would be affected. Without this context, I can't properly assess the full business impact.
